examples.title: Process Creation using fork() in C
examples.description: Demonstrates creating a child process using fork() system call
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates Unix process creation using fork(). The fork() system call duplicates the current process, creating a child. The child gets a copy of the parent's memory (including the count variable). Both processes continue execution after fork(), but the return value differs: 0 for child, child's PID for parent.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is size of parent process memory copied to child

examples.title: Thread Creation in Python
examples.description: Demonstrates creating and managing multiple threads in Python
examples.code:
import threading
import time

# Function to be executed by threads
def print_numbers(thread_name, delay):
    for i in range(1, 6):
        print(f"{thread_name}: {i}")
        time.sleep(delay)

# Main thread execution
print("Main: Creating threads...")

# Create thread objects
thread1 = threading.Thread(target=print_numbers, args=("Thread-1", 0.5))
thread2 = threading.Thread(target=print_numbers, args=("Thread-2", 1.0))

# Start threads
print("Main: Starting Thread-1")
thread1.start()

print("Main: Starting Thread-2")
thread2.start()

# Wait for threads to complete
thread1.join()
thread2.join()

print("Main: All threads completed!")

# Display thread information
print(f"Active threads: {threading.active_count()}")
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates multithreading in Python using the threading module. Each thread executes the print_numbers function concurrently. The join() method ensures the main thread waits for all child threads to complete before exiting. Threads share the same memory space, so they can access and modify shared variables (unlike processes).
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the number of iterations per thread (concurrent execution)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional space per thread for stack

examples.title: Process Scheduling Simulation - Round Robin
examples.description: Simulates Round Robin CPU scheduling algorithm
examples.explanation: This code simulates Round Robin CPU scheduling. Each process gets a fixed time quantum to execute. If a process's remaining burst time is more than the time quantum, it gets preempted and moved to the back of the ready queue. This algorithm ensures fair CPU distribution and is commonly used in time-sharing systems.

examples.title: Process Synchronization using Semaphore
examples.description: Demonstrates producer-consumer problem with semaphore synchronization
examples.code:
import threading
import time
import random

# Shared buffer (bounded buffer of size 5)
buffer = []
BUFFER_SIZE = 5

# Semaphores for synchronization
empty = threading.Semaphore(BUFFER_SIZE)  # Count of empty slots
full = threading.Semaphore(0)             # Count of filled slots
mutex = threading.Lock()                   # Mutex for critical section

def producer(producer_id):
    global buffer
    for i in range(5):
        item = random.randint(1, 100)
        
        # Wait for empty slot
        empty.acquire()
        
        # Enter critical section
        mutex.acquire()
        buffer.append(item)
        print(f"Producer {producer_id} produced: {item}")
        print(f"Buffer: {buffer}")
        mutex.release()
        
        # Signal that buffer has item
        full.release()
        
        time.sleep(random.uniform(0.5, 1.5))

def consumer(consumer_id):
    global buffer
    for i in range(5):
        # Wait for item to be available
        full.acquire()
        
        # Enter critical section
        mutex.acquire()
        item = buffer.pop(0)
        print(f"Consumer {consumer_id} consumed: {item}")
        print(f"Buffer: {buffer}")
        mutex.release()
        
        # Signal that slot is empty
        empty.release()
        
        time.sleep(random.uniform(0.5, 1.5))

# Create and start threads
producer_threads = [threading.Thread(target=producer, args=(i,)) for i in range(2)]
consumer_threads = [threading.Thread(target=consumer, args=(i,)) for i in range(2)]

for t in producer_threads + consumer_threads:
    t.start()

for t in producer_threads + consumer_threads:
    t.join()

print("All producers and consumers finished!")
examples.explanation: This code solves the producer-consumer synchronization problem using semaphores. The empty semaphore tracks empty buffer slots, full tracks filled slots, and mutex provides mutual exclusion for buffer access. Producers wait (decrement) on empty before adding, then signal on full. Consumers do the opposite. This prevents race conditions and ensures bounded buffer limits are respected.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is total items produced/consumed
